content:
# Matthew Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, Quire A, first folio of Matthew | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_001_page_001.jpg, MAT_001_page_002.jpg

1. The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.

2. Abraham fathered Isaac, and Isaac fathered Jacob, and Jacob fathered Judah and his brothers.

3. And Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar, and Perez fathered Hezron, and Hezron fathered Ram.

4. And Ram fathered Amminadab, and Amminadab fathered Nahshon, and Nahshon fathered Salmon.

5. And Salmon fathered Boaz by Rahab, and Boaz fathered Obed by Ruth, and Obed fathered Jesse.

6. And Jesse fathered David the king. And David fathered Solomon by the wife of Uriah.

7. And Solomon fathered Rehoboam, and Rehoboam fathered Abijah, and Abijah fathered Asaph.

8. And Asaph fathered Jehoshaphat, and Jehoshaphat fathered Joram, and Joram fathered Uzziah.

9. And Uzziah fathered Jotham, and Jotham fathered Ahaz, and Ahaz fathered Hezekiah.

10. And Hezekiah fathered Manasseh, and Manasseh fathered Amos, and Amos fathered Josiah.

11. And Josiah fathered Jechoniah and his brothers, at the time of the deportation to Babylon.

12. And after the deportation to Babylon, Jechoniah fathered Shealtiel, and Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel.

13. And Zerubbabel fathered Abiud, and Abiud fathered Eliakim, and Eliakim fathered Azor.

14. And Azor fathered Zadok, and Zadok fathered Achim, and Achim fathered Eliud.

15. And Eliud fathered Eleazar, and Eleazar fathered Matthan, and Matthan fathered Jacob.

16. And Jacob fathered Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.

17. So all the generations from Abraham to David were fourteen generations, and from David to the deportation to Babylon fourteen generations, and from the deportation to Babylon to the Christ fourteen generations.

18. Now the birth of Jesus Christ took place in this way. When his mother Mary had been betrothed to Joseph, before they came together she was found to be with child from the Holy Spirit.

19. And Joseph her husband, being a righteous man and unwilling to put her to public shame, resolved to divorce her quietly.

20. But as he considered these things, behold, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream, saying, "Joseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mary as your wife, for that which has been conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.

21. She will bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins."

22. Now all this took place to fulfill what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet:

23. "Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel," which means, God with us.

24. And Joseph, having risen from sleep, did as the angel of the Lord commanded him and took his wife.

25. And he did not know her until she bore a son. And he called his name Jesus.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Book of the genealogy" (v.1):** The Greek βίβλος γενέσεως echoes the Septuagint's rendering of the Hebrew תּוֹלְדוֹת formula in Genesis (Gen 2:4, 5:1). This phrase positions Matthew's opening as a deliberate parallel to Genesis — a new beginning. I render γενέσεως as "genealogy" rather than "genesis" or "origin" because the immediate function of the passage is genealogical, though the broader resonance with Genesis is intentional on Matthew's part.

2. **"Christ" vs. "Messiah" (throughout):** I render Χριστός consistently as "Christ" in the LOGOS Bible. While the word is the Greek translation of the Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ (Messiah, "Anointed One"), by the time of the NT writings Χριστός was functioning as both a title and an identifier for Jesus. The formal tradition of English Bible translation has maintained "Christ," and LOGOS follows this convention. The titular force is preserved by the article in v.17: "the Christ" (τὸν Χριστόν).

3. **"Fathered" (throughout):** The Greek ἐγέννησεν is rendered consistently as "fathered" rather than the archaic "begat." This maintains the formal register while being accessible. The passive shift at v.16 (ἐγεννήθη, "was born") is thereby made more striking — the active chain of "fathered" is broken precisely where Joseph's biological role is absent.

4. **"Asaph" and "Amos" (vv.7, 10):** These are the readings of Codex Sinaiticus (shared with Vaticanus). Later manuscripts harmonize to "Asa" (the king, 1 Kings 15) and "Amon" (the king, 2 Kings 21). LOGOS follows the Sinaiticus reading as the primary text. Whether these represent variant spellings or deliberate scribal choices that evoke the psalmist Asaph and the prophet Amos is a matter of scholarly debate.

5. **"The wife of Uriah" (v.6):** The Sinaiticus reads τῆς τοῦ Οὐρίου — literally "the [wife/woman] of Uriah." Notably, Bathsheba is never named. This circumlocution may reflect a deliberate theological choice: the focus falls on David's sin against Uriah rather than on Bathsheba herself. LOGOS preserves this construction.

6. **"Betrothed" (v.18):** The Greek μνηστευθείσης refers to the formal Jewish betrothal (erusin), which was legally binding — more than modern "engagement." A betrothed woman was legally a wife, and dissolution required a formal divorce. "Betrothed" preserves the technical formality that LOGOS requires.

7. **"Put her to public shame" (v.19):** The Sinaiticus reads δειγματίσαι (to make an example of, to expose). This is the simpler form; some manuscripts have the intensified παραδειγματίσαι. Both carry the sense of public disgrace. Joseph's righteousness is expressed not in strict legal enforcement but in merciful restraint.

8. **"Behold" (vv.20, 23):** The Greek ἰδού is retained as "Behold" in the LOGOS Bible. While this word has fallen from common English usage, it carries a formal weight and announcement quality that no modern equivalent fully captures. It serves as a marker of divine speech and prophetic declaration.

9. **"The virgin" (v.23):** The Sinaiticus quotes Isaiah 7:14 using the LXX's παρθένος (virgin), which translates the Hebrew עַלְמָה (young woman of marriageable age). This translation choice by the LXX translators is itself theologically significant and is the form Matthew employs. LOGOS translates what the Greek text says.

10. **"He did not know her" (v.25):** The euphemistic use of γινώσκω for sexual relations follows the Hebrew idiom of ידע (yada). LOGOS retains this formal biblical idiom. The Sinaiticus omits "firstborn" (πρωτότοκον), reading simply "a son" (υἱόν) — a shorter and likely earlier reading.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
